draft.introduction: Chapter 2 of Tacitus’s Germania (98 CE) provides the earliest account of Germanic origin myths. Tacitus reports that the Germans sing of a god Tuisto, born from the earth, whose son Mannus fathered the three ancestral tribes.

translation.text: In their ancient songs, which are their only form of record and history, they celebrate a god Tuisto, born from the earth. To him they ascribe a son, Mannus, the origin and founder of their race. To Mannus they assign three sons, from whose names the peoples nearest the Ocean are called Ingaevones, those of the interior Herminones, and the remainder Istaevones.
translation.original_text: Celebrant carminibus antiquis, quod unum apud illos memoriae et annalium genus est, Tuistonem deum terra editum. Ei filium Mannum originem gentis conditoremque. Manno tres filios assignant.

translator_notes.note: The name Tuisto may derive from Proto-Germanic *twistaz (“double” or “twin”), suggesting an androgynous primordial being. Mannus (“man”) parallels the Indian Manu and the common Indo-European pattern of deriving humanity from a primal ancestor whose name simply means “human.”
